EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ef0 tornado started about 3 miles south of Uehling around 527 pm. The tornado tracked to the north northeast damaging some farm buildings, trees and hay stacks.  The tornado ended about 1 mile east of uehling around 532 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moving across Nebraska helped thunderstorm development ahead of a cold front.  There were isolated reports of severe weather with these storms across the eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa region, including one tornado near Uehling Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the Fremont County Sheriff's Department communication tower causing severe damage to nearly everthing electrical in the building.  Total damage to repair or replace the items was estimated at around $100,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level disturbance that tapped tropical moisture brought several days of heavy rain to eastern Nebraska and western Iowa over the weekend of October 12th through the 14th. The heavy rain produced flooding along the Missouri River south of Nebraska City along with flooding tributaries of the Missouri River in southeast Nebraska.|Some locations received over 5 or 6 inches of rain from the system, including a 24 hour total ending Monday morning the 15th of 4.95 inches in Nebraska City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain along the Missouri River and its tributaries in southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa caused the Missouri River to flood in extreme southeast Nebraska from upstream near Peru through Brownville and then down to Rulo.  At Brownville, the Missouri River crested at 33.6 feet for around 4 hours beginning around 115 pm CDT on October 15th.  Flood stage is 32 feet.  The river at Brownville was above flood stage from around 730 am CDT on the 15th until 515 am on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level disturbance that tapped tropical moisture brought several days of heavy rain to eastern Nebraska and western Iowa over the weekend of October 12th through the 14th. The heavy rain produced flooding along the Missouri River south of Nebraska City along with flooding tributaries of the Missouri River in southeast Nebraska.|Some locations received over 5 or 6 inches of rain from the system, including a 24 hour total ending Monday morning the 15th of 4.95 inches in Nebraska City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused the Missouri River to flood from upstream in Nemaha county down through the Rulo area.  The flooding affected mainly low farmlands along the river.  The Missouri River at Rulo reached a crest of 19.8 feet around 615 pm CDT on October 15th.  The crest lasted about 4 hours.  The river at Rulo was above flood stage from around 915 am CDT on the 15th until 1015 am on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stalled upper level system brought 4 straight days of moderate to heavy rainfall over sections of northeast Nebraska.  Around 5 inches of rain was reported around the Humphrey area which produced some minor lowland flooding near and just south of town along Tracey Creek.  The flooding washed some gravel off of county roads and flooded a few low farm fields near town.

Scattered thunderstorms just to the northeast of Guide Rock dumped very heavy rain across eastern Webster County. Rainfall of 2 to 4 inches was common, most of which fell in the Elm, Crooked and Willow Creeks. The runoff caused the Republican River near Guide Rock to overflow its banks for a time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Republican River south of Guide Rock topped its banks shortly after midnight on October 5 and remained in flood until around noon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level disturbance that tapped tropical moisture brought several days of heavy rain to eastern Nebraska and western Iowa over the weekend of October 12th through the 14th. The heavy rain produced flooding along the Missouri River south of Nebraska City along with flooding tributaries of the Missouri River in southeast Nebraska.|Some locations received over 5 or 6 inches of rain from the system, including a 24 hour total ending Monday morning the 15th of 4.95 inches in Nebraska City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flooding along the Little Nemaha River both upstream and downstream of Auburn.  The river at Auburn crested at 22.6 feet around 615 am CDT on October 15th, flood stage is 22 feet.  The river at Auburn was above flood stage from around 1145 pm CDT on 10/14 until 930 am on the 15th.||The flooding closed Highway 136 east of Auburn for around 6 hours and also flooded some farmland along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds moved through portions of Western Nebraska with sustained winds of 40 to 49 mph and gusts to around 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Roads automated observing equipment recorded wind gusts to 62 mph at Big Springs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Department of Roads automated observing equipment measured sustained winds of 45 kts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rumbled across a section of south central Nebraska and produced 60 mph winds. The storms began around Cambridge where a large tree was toppled in the wind. The thunderstorms spread east across Furnas, Phelps and Dawson counties.

Afternoon thunderstorms brought 60 mph winds to parts of central Nebraska.  Some tree limbs were downed by the winds southwest of Belgrade. Other strong wind reports came from the Silver Creek and Shelby areas.

Thunderstorms south of Hastings produced some hail and eventually high winds as they roared east into Clay and Fillmore counties. The thunderstorms formed just behind a cold front. At one point, there as a report of a tornado near Blue Hill, but off-duty National Weather Service personnel in the area reported just some blowing dust due to high winds. Those high winds spread east across southern Clay and southwest Fillmore counties where 60 to 70 mph winds were reported. No significant damage was observed.

A surface front located across the Sandhills provided focus for outbreak of severe weather including numerous reports of tornadoes. Strong storms also dropped out of South Dakota and quickly turned tornadic. Additional storms moved out of Northeast Colorado and brought large hail to Southwest Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down a few miles north of Cody and moved south.  In the path, the tornado tore the roof off a shop, destroyed a barn, twisted iron feed bunks, and snapped trees in a wind break. The main household was not damaged and no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms, a few severe, developed along a slow moving cold front during the late afternoon of September 24th.  The storms produced isolated wind damage and large hail in Nebraska.  One super-cell thunderstorm developed behind the cold front late in the evening, aided by an upper level disturbance lifting out of Wyoming.  This storm produced scattered large hail reports from the David City area northeast to west of North Bend.

A couple of rounds of thunderstorms impacted south central Nebraska on this day. The first was in the morning hours. A severe thunderstorm rolled east across the Wolbach area. High winds dropped power lines and large trees in town. The storm ripped right across Nance County where 60 mph winds were common, as was tree damage and power line damage along with some minor property damage. Another round of high winds hit the area late in the day, this time from west of Holdrege through Hastings. Once again, 60 mph winds were common with trees and power lines damaged. The U.S. Highway 6 and 34 corridor west of Hastings was hardest hit. Elsewhere, up to 6 inches of rain pounded Polk County. The Davis Creek, Prairie Creek and Big Blue River were all in flood for a time. Damage to county roads was common place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of streets in town and nearby county roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down and windows in a house were blown in at a location 6 miles west and 3 miles south of Decatur.  Winds estimated to be over 80 mph.

A closed upper level low pressure system that tracked across northern Nebraska triggered a few severe thunderstorms across northeast Nebraska and parts of western Iowa.  Temperatures in advance of the system climbed into the upper 70s to lower 80s with dew point readings in the lower 60s, and temperatures behind the system were in the 50s.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1 inch in diameter lasted for around 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that developed over central Nebraska earlier that day pushed southeast across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa during the late afternoon and early evening hours of August 12th.  Despite a large scale upper ridge in place across the region, the storms became severe as they tapped a large pocket of instability over eastern Nebraska.  The storms developed a large cold pool and produced scattered areas of strong and damaging winds as several bow echoes moved across the area.||In addition, torrential rain occurred within the strongest part of the complex with near zero visibilities causing conditions to become like nightfall prompting street lights to come on.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were downed in town due to thunderstorm winds estimated at around 70 mph.  Several windows in town were blown out by the winds along with some door, roof and vehicle damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight surface pressure gradient developed as a strong low pressure system moved into Western Iowa.  This resulted in strong northwest winds of 40 mph with gusts of 55 to 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds of 40 mph with gusts to 60 mph occurred across Arthur County between noon and 2 pm CST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds of 40 mph with gusts to 59 mph occurred across Chase County between noon and 2 pm CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms produced a few severe hail events during the early evening hours and then later into the evening the storms produced severe winds.
An isolated thunderstorm dropped severe hail over southwest Custer County and northeast Lincoln County during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the hail, 1.34 inches of  rain fell over the past hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm dropped severe hail over southwest Custer County and northeast Lincoln County during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The thunderstorm produced hail off and on for the last 30 minutes.

Thunderstorms moved across mainly northern LBF CWA with severe hail and wind reports and an isolated tornado confirmed across Custer county. Numerous reports of wind damage received with gusts up to 70 knots estimated by storm chaser.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds uprooted and broke off numerous large trees, tore off part of the roof of the Local Pride packing plant, took down power lines, and damaged the siding and sign for the Ideal grocery store on the south side of Gordon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second day in a row a stationary front extended across extreme southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa during the afternoon of August 8th.  Conditions along and south of the boundary were very unstable with temperatures well into the 90s and dew point temperatures in the mid and upper 70s.  Scattered thunderstorms...a few severe with heavy rain...developed along the boundary during the late afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of around 2 inches in an hour or so caused flooding across Highway 73 north of Falls City closing it for a while this evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact upper low pressure system tracked across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa early in the afternoon on 3/31/07.  Just ahead of this system, from extreme eastern Nebraska into western Iowa, partial sunshine allowed temperatures to climb to around 70 degrees with dewpoints in the 50s, while a bit farther to the west in the Columbus and Norfolk areas, readings were in the 40s.  The contrast in temperatures and dynamics from the upper low helped kick off severe thunderstorms across the area, including at least one short-lived tornado west of Herman, Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down 3 miles west of Herman around 235 pm CDT and tracked about 1 mile before lifting according to a NWS storm survey.  The tornado's path was around 1/4 mile wide as it damaged some sheds and almost destroyed a horse barn.  The tornado also snapped a few trees in the area and did some fence damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid snow melt on frozen soils caused minor flooding along small streams in Knox county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid snow melt caused flooding of small streams in Knox county.  Bazile Creek, near Maiden's Leap, 5 miles east of Niobrara, flooded a few county roads and Highway 12 causing their closure.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa ahead of a strong upper level disturbance that moved across South Dakota.  The storms fired up in western Nebraska late in the afternoon and moved into extreme eastern Nebraska toward midnight CDT.  Although there was a warm front over far southeast Nebraska...the storms were mostly tied into the upper level feature.

Severe thunderstorms moved across Cherry into Brown County during the afternoon and early evening hours. Hail up to the size of baseballs was reported along with numerous reports of damaging winds from these storms.
Several thunderstorms formed over Central Nebraska during the afternoon hours with isolated reports of nickel to golf-ball sized hail across Wheeler County.

Thunderstorms over the weekend dumped up to 5 inches of rain across parts of south-central Nebraska. Just over five inches (5.07) fell in the Grand Island area and around four inches (4.14) fell in Aurora. This caused flooding in the northwest part of Grand Island and through parts of Aurora. Many roads were closed in these towns.  Rural areas of Clay and Fillmore Counties also saw heavy rains and flooding of mainly rural roadways.
A thunderstorm produces quarter size hail in Dakota County of extreme northeast Nebraska during the late afternoon of September 30th.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail and heavy rainfall across much of  NE Colorado, NW Kansas and SW Nebraska.  Hail up to tennis ball size cracked the windshield of a NWS co-op observer's car in Kit Carson county.

Severe thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours and continued throughout the morning producing several reports of hail and some flash flooding. During the afternoon, tornadic supercells developed leading to reports of tornadoes, hail and flash flooding which continued into the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported water over State Spur Route 556A just north of Fort McPherson National Cemetery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms produced numerous severe hail events during the late afternoon and evening hours.

A warm front that was along the Kansas and Nebraska border early Saturday morning May 5th, lifted north during the day bringing widespread heavy rain and severe weather, including tornadoes, to eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  The warm front was part of a large upper level system that brought several days of severe weather and flooding to the region. |Heavy rain and flooding was reported with some of the activity Saturday morning, along with isolated severe thunderstorm reports.  However, during the afternoon the severe weather became more widespread over northeast Nebraska where supercells spawned several tornadoes.  At this time, thunderstorms, some severe with heavy rain, produced flash flooding over parts of southeast Nebraska.  By evening the severe weather, including supercell producing tornadoes, shifted into southwest Iowa, although isolated severe thunderstorms persisted over parts of eastern Nebraska until after midnight cdt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado did intermittent damage from 2 miles south of Western to around 3 miles southeast of Dorchester.  As the tornado initially touched down south of Western, a camper and machine shed were destroyed and a center pivot overturned.  In rural Saline county around 13 miles west of Wilber trees were twisted and uprooted and some building damage was noted and some debris was scattered up to 1/4 mile away from its original location.  Farm outbuildings were severely damaged in the Tabor Hall area, which is 5 miles south of Dorchester, and numerous trees were uprooted or destroyed in the area which destroyed or severely damaged 8 headstones and monuments in a nearby cemetery.  The final damage area was around 3 miles southeast of Dorchester where several pig feeders and livestock huts were destroyed and the roof of a farm outbuilding was removed and a barn destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical airmass and weak instability combined to help produce a funnel cloud southwest of Aurora. Also, heavy rains were noted in the Merrick, Polk, Nance, Furnas and Gosper county areas.

Isolated severe thunderstorms produced strong and damaging winds across Webster and Fillmore counties in south central Nebraska. The most damage was at the Webster County seat of Red Cloud. Several large trees were damaged or fallen. At least two residences had broken windows and there were reports of minor roof damage to outbuildings. At Exeter in Fillmore County, tree damage was also widespread.

Severe storms initiated over Wheeler and Garfield Counties of North Central Nebraska during early evening hours on July 8th, producing nickel to golf ball sized hail. Additional storms moved into Southwest Nebraska by mid to late evening and a few isolated severe storms produced wind gusts of 52 knots. Heavy rainfall fell over flash flood prone areas of Frontier County with numerous reports of over 3 inches of rainfall received.

Severe thunderstorms initiated over the Eastern Sandhills in North Central Nebraska in the late afternoon hours, then track eastward through the mid evening hours. Numerous reports of hail in the 1 to 2 inch range were received with one report of baseball sized hail in Northeast Custer County.
Scattered thunderstorms moved across south-central Nebraska in the late afternoon and evening hours. These storms produced some dime to quarter sized hail and some good rains.

A warm front that was along the Kansas and Nebraska border early Saturday morning May 5th, lifted north during the day bringing widespread heavy rain and severe weather, including tornadoes, to eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  The warm front was part of a large upper level system that brought several days of severe weather and flooding to the region. |Heavy rain and flooding was reported with some of the activity Saturday morning, along with isolated severe thunderstorm reports.  However, during the afternoon the severe weather became more widespread over northeast Nebraska where supercells spawned several tornadoes.  At this time, thunderstorms, some severe with heavy rain, produced flash flooding over parts of southeast Nebraska.  By evening the severe weather, including supercell producing tornadoes, shifted into southwest Iowa, although isolated severe thunderstorms persisted over parts of eastern Nebraska until after midnight cdt.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado initially touched down about 1.5 miles southeast of Bloomfield where it flipped a center pivot.  Tree and outbuilding damage was noted for several miles north of that point, including a few dead livestock.  The damage path became less clear north of town, but some minor debris was noted until about 4 miles north of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the mid afternoon across southwest and portions of the panhandle in Nebraska.  Some of these storms produced severe hail.
Supercell thunderstorms produced large hail across western and north central Nebraska during the late afternoon and evening hours.

A large upper level disturbance over the Rocky Mountains combined with a nearly stationary front over southeast Nebraska brought strong to severe thunderstorms to parts of the area for the second night in a row.  This time the stronger storms were generally confined to southeast Nebraska where a multicell cluster of storms bowed out across southeast Nebraska.  The storms did produce at least one weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds of 60 to 70 mph were estimated by emergency management south of Beatrice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of severe thunderstorms formed along a cold front that moved across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the late afternoon and evening hours on March 21.  Temperatures climbed into the 70s to around 80 degrees in advance of the front, and dew-points rose into the upper 50s to lower 60s.  This provided the necessary instability to help touch off the severe thunderstorms.  The storms produced mainly nickel to quarter size hail...although a few larger stones were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 3/4 inches in diameter was reported by the public at the Bay Hills Golf Course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of rounds of thunderstorms impacted south central Nebraska on this day. The first was in the morning hours. A severe thunderstorm rolled east across the Wolbach area. High winds dropped power lines and large trees in town. The storm ripped right across Nance County where 60 mph winds were common, as was tree damage and power line damage along with some minor property damage. Another round of high winds hit the area late in the day, this time from west of Holdrege through Hastings. Once again, 60 mph winds were common with trees and power lines damaged. The U.S. Highway 6 and 34 corridor west of Hastings was hardest hit. Elsewhere, up to 6 inches of rain pounded Polk County. The Davis Creek, Prairie Creek and Big Blue River were all in flood for a time. Damage to county roads was common place.

Severe thunderstorms produced a widespread area of large hail and high winds from Gosper County to Sherman County. Some of the worst damage was along the Dawson and Gosper counties line. In this area, the combination of 40 to 60 mph winds and hail to the size of golf balls battered crops. There were several reports of tree limbs down around the lake and in the town of Elwood. The crow's nest (i.e. announcer's booth) at the Elwood football field was blown apart by the wind. Though not as prolific, the hail was generally bigger to the northeast in northwest Buffalo County, and in Sherman County about halfway between Rockville and Loup City, where baseball size hail was reported on Highway 58.

Warm and humid air aloft combined with a stationary front over southern Nebraska and caused scattered thunderstorms to develop over northeast Nebraska during the predawn hours of August 22nd.  A few of these storms turned severe over northeast Nebraska and extreme western Iowa a little after sunrise.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to golf ball size was accompanied by heavy rain with 2.00 inches in 60 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms produced a few severe hail events during the early evening hours and then later into the evening the storms produced severe winds.

A cluster of thunderstorms developed across western Nebraska in the early evening hours of April 2nd.  The cluster of storms transitioned into a line of storms that produced severe winds and hail across southwest Nebraska.

Thunderstorms developed across south-central Nebraska. These storms produced widespread rains and some hail. Nearly the entire region picked up an inch or more of rain. From Beaver City and Phillipsburg Kansas to St. Paul and Aurora picked up over 2 inches. The most rain fell in Holdrege where they picked up three and two-thirds inches of rain.  These rains fell across an area that had received several inches of rain over the past 3 days.  The runoff from these storms filled up ditches and roadways across the area. Several low areas became flooded.  Some hail did accompany the storms in which most were less than an inch in diameter.

Scattered thunderstorms rumbled across south-central Nebraska. These storms produced some isolated severe events. Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported near Henderson in York County. In addition, hail around the size of quarters fell near Ong in Clay County and near Lawrence in Nuckolls County.  No significant damage was reported.
A severe thunderstorm moved across parts of the northern Nebraska panhandle and produced some large hail and strong winds.

Late morning thunderstorms continued across south-central Nebraska into the evening hours. Some of these storms became severe and produced hail up to the size of golf balls and wind gusts estimated at 60 to 65 mph.  The first severe reports came in just before the noon hour of hail in Nance County between Fullerton and Genoa. Most storms had exited the area by mid-afternoon.  Additional storms developed in the eastern areas in the evening and primarily produced gusty winds.

Thunderstorms that developed over central Nebraska earlier that day pushed southeast across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa during the late afternoon and early evening hours of August 12th.  Despite a large scale upper ridge in place across the region, the storms became severe as they tapped a large pocket of instability over eastern Nebraska.  The storms developed a large cold pool and produced scattered areas of strong and damaging winds as several bow echoes moved across the area.||In addition, torrential rain occurred within the strongest part of the complex with near zero visibilities causing conditions to become like nightfall prompting street lights to come on.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust of 58 mph was measured by wind equipment near 150th and Fort Streets.  The strong winds spread across the Omaha area causing scattered tree damage including on large tree that fell on 2 cars near the 4600 block on South 34th Street.  Also a tree fell across South 15th Street near the 1900 block blocking traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms, a few severe, developed along a slow moving cold front during the late afternoon of September 24th.  The storms produced isolated wind damage and large hail in Nebraska.  One super-cell thunderstorm developed behind the cold front late in the evening, aided by an upper level disturbance lifting out of Wyoming.  This storm produced scattered large hail reports from the David City area northeast to west of North Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to one inch in diameter damaged vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front draped across southern Nebraska and an upper level disturbance helped kick off heavy rain producing thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours of August 22nd.  The thunderstorms produced some flash flooding, isolated wind damage and isolated marginal severe size hail.
Thunderstorms that developed along a weak slow moving cold front produced isolated wind damage across parts of eastern Nebraska during the very early morning hours of July 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at over 60 mph blew a few trees down across a county road near Octavia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing hail, high winds and heavy rain pounded a part of south central Nebraska. The thunderstorms were most intense along the U.S. Highway 81 corridor from York to Hebron. In York, very heavy rain flooded some streets, including the Lincoln Avenue underpass. A few cars were temporarily stranded in the high water. To the south, it wasn't just heavy rain in Fillmore County. The Emergency Manager reported a funnel cloud for a brief time east of Geneva. Dime to quarter size hail fell on the west side of Geneva and 60 mph winds befell the Ohiowa area.

A swath of crop damage, overturned pivots and minor property damage was the result of severe thunderstorms, which rumbled across south central Nebraska. The thunderstorms moved east and southeast with the most significant damage in southern Gosper and northeast Furnas counties. In the boot heel of Gosper County, crops were ravaged by golf ball size hail and 70 mph winds. At least two pivots were overturned. The storm responsible for the Gosper County damage took a southeast turn and hammered the Oxford area. Large trees fell throughout the town of Oxford and baseball size hail was reported west of town. Several pivots were overturned by the wind. This storm weakened just a bit, but still managed to produce 60 mph winds and nickel size hail in much of Franklin County. Earlier in the evening, a lonesome thunderstorm in the Holdrege area produced golf ball size southeast of town.
